videos.title: Uzbekistan Tour Guide | A-Z India to Uzbekistan Plan, Tourist Places, Itinerary & BUDGET in Hindi
videos.description:
#uzbekistan #indiatouzbekistan #tashkent 

Uzbekistan Tour Guide | A-Z India to Tashkent Uzbekistan Plan, Tourist Places, Itinerary & BUDGET

Discover the Hidden Gems of Uzbekistan in 2024!

Embark on a captivating journey through the ancient Silk Road with our comprehensive Uzbekistan tour guide for 2024. Experience the rich history, stunning architecture, and vibrant culture of this Central Asian gem.

In this video, you'll explore:

Iconic Landmarks: Marvel at the majestic Registan Square in Samarkand, the ancient city of Bukhara, and the stunning Gur-e-Amir mausoleum.
Cultural Treasures: Immerse yourself in the intricate art and architecture of Uzbekistan, including the vibrant bazaars, traditional music, and delicious cuisine.
Hidden Gems: Discover off-the-beaten-path destinations like the Khiva Old Town, the Fergana Valley, and the stunning Charvak Reservoir.
Travel Tips: Get practical advice on planning your trip, including visa requirements, transportation options, accommodation, and budgeting.
Whether you're a history buff, a culture enthusiast, or simply seeking a unique adventure, this video has everything you need to plan your unforgettable Uzbekistan tour in 2024.

Don't miss out on this incredible opportunity! Subscribe to our channel for more travel inspiration and informative videos.

#Uzbekistan #TravelGuide #CentralAsia #SilkRoad #Tourism #Adventure #Explore #Vacation #Trip #BucketList #MustSee #TravelTips #2024 #History #Culture #Architecture #Food #TravelBlogger #TravelVlogger

videos.title: How To Spend 2 Days In TASHKENT Uzbekistan | Travel Itinerary
videos.description:
In this 2-day Tashkent travel guide, I’m taking you through the perfect itinerary where cab rides can cost around $3, metro rides are basically pocket change, and you can eat legendary local food like plov for the price of a coffee. You’ll explore Silk Road landmarks, Soviet-era architecture, and jaw-dropping modern spots—then finish Day 2 in the mountains at Ugam-Chatkal National Park with stops at Charvak Reservoir, Chimgan Village, and Beldersay. Expect insider tips, exact costs, and the easiest ways to get around using Yandex Go and the beautiful Tashkent Metro.

💲*To streamline your travel planning, we've converted all local currency prices into USD. This simple step means easy budgeting for our international travelers, ensuring a smooth and enjoyable trip without the fuss of currency calculations!*

videos.transcript: I found a capital city where cab rides cost $3 and meals barely touch your wallet. Sounds fake, right? This is Tesh Kent, a city where deep history meets bold modern design and living well costs way less than you'd expect. >> [music] >> I'll show you how exactly to get around, what to eat, and how to travel it like a pro. Day one, Silk Road roots and Soviet skies. Morning. Corsu Bazaar. We start our day in the capital at the legendary Corsu Bazaar. Its massive turquoise dome rises above the old town, housing a maze of vendors selling mounds of red spices, dried apricots, and even handpainted ceramics. I recommend heading to the outer rim to find the non-bakers first, watching them pull hot stamped bread from the clay ovens as a show in itself, and a steaming loaf costs around 50ents. It's the perfect snack to eat while you browse. Afterward, check out the stalls of colorful e-cot fabrics or traditional knives. Vendors here expect a bit of bargaining, so don't hesitate to negotiate a fair price. Insider tip: Take the stairs to the second floor gallery. While the ground floor is packed, the upper level offers a clean look at the complex geometry of the counters. You get a full scale of the operation here without the crowd. Hazerati Imam Complex. A short ride brings us to the Hazerati Imam complex. This sprawling square acts as the religious heart of the capital. Surrounded by towering minouetses and madrasas, the architecture here defines the Silk Road style, mixing detailed brick work and bright blue domes. You need to find the small Moy Mubarak library within the grounds. It preserves the Uman Kuran, one of the oldest manuscripts in existence. Written on giant deer skin pages from the seventh century. Entry is roughly a dollar and standing inches away from history. This significance is an experience you shouldn't skip. Insider tip. Check out the Barack Khan Madrasa on the side of the square. The former student cells now house local artisans so you can watch masters carving wood and painting miniatures right in their workshops. It connects you with the craft. Much better than a standard store. Late morning independent square. From the old town, make your way to the modern center at Musta Kalik Medoni. This enormous square covers nearly 30 acres and functions as the administrative heart of the nation. You're looking at a space defined by white marble columns, endless fountains, and the Senate building in the background. I suggest walking through the arch of good and noble aspirations topped with gold storks to reach the central monument. A massive globe and a statue of a mother holding a child now stand where the Soviet leaders once did. It costs nothing to enter and the shaded paths provide a necessary break from the city's heat. Afternoon Air Teamer Square. Head straight to the geographical center of modern Teshk. Amir Teamer Square is a manicured park dominated by the bronze statue of the conqueror himself on horseback. The history here is fascinating. The same pedestal once held statues of Stalin and Marx before the nation reclaimed its identity. The view behind the statue is just as famous. You're staring right at the Hotel Usbekiststan, a massive brutalist structure that looks like an open concrete book. It's one of the most photographed Soviet era buildings in the country. I recommend taking a loop around the park to see the contrast between the imperial history and the modern forum palace next door. Lunch Cafe 1991. Walk a few minutes from the square to find Cafe 1991. This restaurant takes traditional usspec recipes and serves them in a polished modern setting. It's the best spot to see how the local cuisine stands up in a fine dining environment. I strongly suggest ordering the crispy eggplant salad to start. The mix of hot fried eggplant with sweet tomatoes is incredible. For a mane, try the tea house pl. It uses darker carrots and sheep fat for a deeper flavor than the street version. A meal here costs around $20 per person, which is fair given the excellent quality. The underground commute. To reach your next stop, forget the taxi and head underground. The Tashkent Metro isn't just a transport. The Soviets designed every single station here to look and feel like a royal palace, also called Palace for the People, using real art and expensive stone to decorate the walls. Jump on at a mere teamer square, which feels more like a grand ballroom than a train platform. But the real highlight is when you get off. You're exiting at Cosmanov Tar, and it's a total trip. The whole station's dedicated to the space program with walls that fade from Earth blue to deep space black. As you walk the platforms, you'll pass floating portraits of famous cosmonauts, making the commute feel like a retro sci-fi movie. It's an experience you simply can't get in a cab, and it costs about 13. Museum of Applied Arts. As you exit the station, make your way to the Museum of Applied Arts. It's tucked away in a quiet neighborhood inside the former palace of a Russian diplomat. The building itself is the first thing you notice. The walls and ceilings are covered in stunningly intricate wood carvings and bright handpainted tiles. The collection covers the full range of USC craftsmanship. You'll walk past cases filled with heavy silver jewelry, blue ceramics from Rishan, and the famous gold threaded robes used by Amir. I suggest spending extra time in the main hall to look at the Suzanis. These massive embroidered tapestries are the centerpiece of the museum. Tickets cost around $4, and since the space is compact, you can easily see it all in about 45 minutes. Insider tip: The gift shop in the courtyard is excellent. Unlike the bazaar, the quality here is certified and prices are fixed, so it's a stress-free place to buy genuine silk or a painted plate. Late afternoon, Magic City. After the museum, it's time to deliver on that promise of modern flash. Jump in a taxi and head to Magic City. If Corsu Bazaar is the soul of old Tashkant, this place is the face of the new generation. It's essentially a massive open air theme park and shopping district designed to look like the greatest hits of Europe. You'll walk down the streets that mimic Paris, London, and Amsterdam, complete with a Disneystyle castle that sits on a central lake. It might sound kitschy, but the execution is surprisingly high quality, and it's packed with locals, not just tourists. For a quick break, duck into the Magic Aquarium, the first of its kind in Central Asia. It's a surreal experience to see sharks and stingrays swimming in a tunnel beneath you in a double landlocked country. If you're not into the fish, you can just grab a coffee at one of the lakeside terraces. This is the best spot in the capital to people watch and see the modern youth culture of Usbekiststan. It's vibrant, loud, and completely different from the history you saw this morning. You don't need a ticket to enter the park itself. Walking the streets is free. You only pay for the specific attractions like the aquarium or the rides. Early evening, Tash Kent Television Tower. Jump in a taxi and head north to Tash Kent Television Tower. This steel giant stands over,200 ft tall and looks exactly like a retrofuturistic rocket ready to launch. It's one of the tallest structures in Central Asia, and you can see it from almost anywhere in the city. You go here for the altitude. A high-speed elevator shoots you up to the observation deck, where the entire city grid lays out below you. On a clear day, you can even spot the Kim Gun Mountains in the distance. Entry costs around $8, and you can hang around the revolving level to watch the 360° view while the floor slowly turns beneath you. Insider tip, you absolutely must bring your physical passport. Security here treats the tower as a strategic site and frequently denies entry to anyone without an ID. So, do not risk leaving it at your hotel. Evening dinner at Beson. Since you're already at the tower, just walk over to Beson to finish the day. This place is legendary for feeding half the city, and the vibe is loud and fun. This isn't a quiet candle lit dinner spot. It's a culinary factory. You'll see waiters sprinting with stacks of plates, and the noise levels intense. You might even share a table with a local family, which is honestly the best way to experience hospitality. You need to try the wedding pla or Toyoshi. It brings a great balance of savory and sweet with chickpeas, raisins, and quail eggs piled on top. For a drink, definitely go for a pot of hot black tea with lemon. It's a local trick to help digest all that heavy rice. Make sure you stop by the open kitchen before you eat. Seeing the cooks working over iron pots the size of a jacuzzi is a sight that really sticks with you. A huge plate runs you about $5, so you can feast like a king for the price of a coffee. Day two, mountain peaks and Tash Kent City Park. Morning, Ugam Chhatkal National Park. On the second day, head out of the city for Ugam Chhatkal National Park. This is a massive protected area in the western Tianan Mountains and it feels worlds away from the busy streets of Teskent. It's wild, green, and absolutely stunning. The driving itself is part of the adventure. As you leave the city limits, the landscape shifts dramatically. You trade the flat, dusty plains for rolling green foothills dotted with fruit orchards and roadside vendors selling honey and dried mountain herbs. Keep your camera ready because the road winds along the Chic River, offering incredible views before you even hit the park entrance. I strongly recommend you hire a private driver for this. If you rely on public transport, you'll struggle to move between the scattered sites and you simply won't be able to cover the whole loop in one day. A private car runs about $50 to $80 and you'll have the flexibility to explore the area at your own pace. Charvak Reservoir. You need about 2 hours to reach your first stop, the Charvac Reservoir. This massive artificial lake sits at the intersection of three wild mountain rivers and is the absolute center of summer life in the region. You'll spot the water immediately. It's a distinct bright turquoise caused by mineral sediment from the glaciers. I recommend you head down to the shoreline called pyramids or the public beaches. Entry fees are low, usually under $3, but if you want to rent a jet ski, be ready to drop about $25. Insider tip. Just keep in mind that if you visit in winter, it's strictly a photo stop. The water level drops and it's far too cold to swim, but the snowy views are still worth a look. Afternoon, Chiman Village. Keep driving up the road until you hit Chiman Village, the unofficial outdoor headquarters for the whole region. This spot feels like a time capsule of a classic mountain resort, and it's chaotic in the best way possible. You'll see the road lined with locals offering rides. So, don't just look, get involved. Hop on a horse or grab an ATV to tear around the valley floor. It's the perfect way to feel like a nomad for 20 minutes. Expect to pay around $5 to $10. But here's the golden rule. Always agree on the price before you put a foot in the stirrup. If you want the high ground, head for the chairlift. It's in a vintage openair Soviet seat that slowly lifts you to the slope. It might look a little rusty, but for just $2, the panoramic view of the greater Chiman Peak is an [music] absolute steal. Lunch at Balanda. Now, let's talk food. Before you leave the village area, pull into Bala. It's a proper mountain lodge, which means you get a real table, a clean restroom, and a killer view from the terrace. The kitchen cranks out solid local classics. Definitely go for the grilled lamb skewers or a hot bowl of pelmen soup. It costs a little more than the street stalls, maybe $10 to $15, but the comfort is absolutely worth the extra cash before we head to the big slopes. Belersai. Drive just 10 minutes from Chingen to find Beldersai. While the crowds stand back at the village, this spot is about the long way up. You have to try the cable car here. It's a beast compared to the last one. A full 3 km stretch that takes you almost half an hour one way. You glide silently over the river and deep into the mountains, which feels completely different from the busy valley floor. Tickets are a bargain at around $3 to $4. You're sitting in a vintage open seat with nothing but a simple bar across your lap. So, if heights make you nervous, keep your eyes fixed on the horizon. For everyone else, it's the best seat in the house. Evening dinner at Quadro. Head back into the city and drive straight into Quadro for a complete change of pace. This restaurant shows off the modern upscale side of Tashkent with a sleek design and a polished atmosphere. The kitchen focuses heavily on European cuisine, and they do it very well. You can expect excellent steaks, fresh seafood, and refined pastas. It's a great spot to sit back with a glass of wine and enjoy a proper high service meal after a dusty day in the mountains. Prices here are higher than the average local spot, usually running between $30 and $50 per person, but the quality of the food and the setting is well worth the money. Tash Kent City Park. End your night at Tesh Kent City Park. This massive complex sits smack in the middle of the new district, surrounded by the Hilton and the glass towers. Head straight to the central lake and catch the musical fountain show. The performance runs every hour from 6:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. It's a chaotic mix of lasers, lights, and water jets shooting 80 meters into the sky in sync with the beat. Grab a spot on the central bridge or the amphitheater steps 5 minutes early so you have a clear view. For a final kick, look for the white futuristic dome nearby. That is the flying theater. Pay about $5 and strap into a hanging seat and take a virtual flight over the country complete with wind and mist hitting your face. Otherwise, just cruise the wide paths and enjoy the lights. Useful info for spending 2 days in Tesh Kent. Best time to visit. Plan your trip for April through May or come in September and October. The air feels great sitting between 65 and 80° F or 18 to 27°. It's the best time to walk the parks and see the sightes comfortably. July and August are very hot. Winter's chilly around 30 to 50° F or negative1 to 10° C which means no crowds and plenty of space. City airport tips. Your arrival point will be Islam Karimov Tash Kent International Airport located just 4 miles or 6 km from the city center. For the fastest entry, use the Yandex Go app to book a car immediately. This ride takes only 15 minutes and costs about $3. If you need a bargain, the public bus is your economical option at roughly 15. Alternatively, skip the app with the official taxi desk, which charges a fixed stress-free transfer fee of roughly $8 straight to your hotel. Where to stay in Tash Kent? When picking your base in Tash Kent, target the neighborhoods that define the city's split personality. Midobod is a solid choice, placing you right next to a mere teamer square, the main parks, and the modern glass skyline. For a completely different energy, focus on Yakasoi, known for its treelined streets, relaxed cafe culture, and exceptional evening dining scene. If you're watching your spending, shared hostel beds begin around $15 nightly. For independent rooms, mid-range hotels typically require an investment starting at $50 with average rates often climbing toward $80 per night. How to get around Tesh Kent. Tash Kent's transport grid is robust, relying on a clean metro system, buses, and cheap app-based taxis. For payment, forget cash entirely. You can now use a contactless bank card or smartphone on all buses and metro turn styles. A single tap validates your ride and costs just 1,700 sum, which is about 13. If you pay cash for a single-use QR ticket at a kiosk, the price jumps to 3,000 sum or roughly 24 cents. Taxis are another major option. Download Yandex Go and skip the street hailing. Rides across town rarely cost more than three or$4. Where to eat in Tash Kent? Tashkent serves up incredible food across its varied neighborhoods. To taste the city's history, drive into the Bodumor district for the central Asian plent. This giant hall cooks the national dish in massive cauldrons offering an authentic bustling lunch experience where a heaping plate costs just $3 to $4. Switch up the vibe on trendy Taras Chevchenko Street at Asona. This sleek venue reimagines local classics with a modern touch, providing a polished atmosphere where dinner bills land between 10 and $15. If you want a dedicated meat-free menu, seek out Eco Cafe. Situated in the quiet Mirzo Ulugbeck district, it stands out as a rare health focused spot serving creative vegetarian plates for roughly $6 to $10 per person. You just packed the absolute best of the capital into 48 hours. You've now seen the underground art galleries, eaten the best rice dish on Earth, and figured out how to travel like a king for pennies. If you liked this deep dive into the city, hit the like button and subscribe for more real itineraries.

videos.title: 11 things you must know about travelling to Uzbekistan in 2025 - A Local and Tourist's Perspective
videos.description:
Before You Visit Uzbekistan in 2025, Watch This!

Planning a trip to Uzbekistan? Whether you're visiting Samarkand, Tashkent, Bukhara, or Khiva, these 11 must-know travel tips will help you get the best experience. This video combines a tourist’s first impressions with local insights, covering everything from travelling there, police requirements, money, culture, and quirky Uzbek things you should know.

0:00 - Introduction
1:15 - 1. Getting to Uzbekistan
1:57 - 2. Uzbekistan Geography
2:12 - 3. Good Hospitality Bad Customer Service
3:03 - 4. Level of English in Uzbekistan
4:49 - 5. Police Registration Requirement
5:52 - 6. Historic Uzbekistan
6:27 - 7. Quiet Tourist Attractions
7:21 - 8. Uzbekistan is CHEAP
8:03 - 9. Yandex Taxi App
8:57 - 10. CRAZY Uzbek Driving
10:47 - 11. Chevrolets in Uzbekistan
12:10 - Closing Thoughts

videos.transcript: usbekistan what a place once the world kept secret on the famous Silk Road now in 2025 is trying to shine on the world stage the USB government has been massively pushing tourism over the last few years with tourism numbers jumping from a few hundred th000 in the early 2000s to a respectable 7.3 million in 2024 the USB government aims to increase tourism to 50 million a year by 2030 published stories and numbers by worldbank.org put the number of visitors to usbekistan higher than Brazil and not far behind Greece not too shabby at all considering most people could not point it out on a map this is a far cry from the days after OB bistan became independent when the country was effectively closed off due to strict visa requirements and poor infrastructure there is a good chance if you are watching this video you are planning a trip to usbekistan in 2025 and this video is designed to help you learn more about the country you were visiting and point out some quirky things you might not be expecting whilst DEA the other half of the Joyce adventurers now lives in the UK she spent her entire childhood in usbekistan and we visited for 3 weeks in late summer 2024 you can watch the Vlogs from our visit here on the channel so consider this a local and a tourist perspective in one go so without further Ado here are 11 things you should know about usbekistan number one we are assuming if you are watching this video you are at least a flight away so how do you actually get here usbekistan has a major International Airport in tashkin we recommend you fly to this airport if you are visiting the country through the national airline USC Airways you might even be able to get get a direct flight from your country of origin the airline is very reasonably priced and offers an exciting start to your adventure in usbekistan you'll be sitting with many USC people watch our travel Vlog listed below to see our USBC Airways experience using the airline you will also start your usbeck adventure in tashkin which is definitely a soft Landing it is the most tourist friendly city in the country and will give you a good base to move on to other destinations like sand and bukara number two usbekistan is a double land country this is everyone's favorite fact about usbekistan you need to know it before you go meaning the country itself is landlocked and the countries around it are also landlocked this sadly means no ocean so yeah leave your swimsuit at home number three we say usbekistan has good Hospitality but bad customer service if you make friends with an obbc person or you stay at a local in or hotel they will take incredible care of you and you will feel like a true VIP in their home or their space don't be surprised whilst you're in the country if you are welcome to someone's table and are fed to death on copious amounts of hot plv or rbec dumplings you will love it but maybe your stomach won't but also don't get too shocked if you go into a restaurant or basically ignored for the whole experience except for when they take your food order and yeah definitely when the bill comes I'll want your attention then Western expectations when it comes to customer service especially if you're American or British could go disappointed this is not just a T's perspective as well dealer agre agrees that really high quality customer service is not part of the culture number four do they speak a word of the Kings and as beckan no not really well if this is it old boy I hope you don't mind if I go out speaking the kings in dealer's family only herself and her sister speak fluent English and having visited the country the fact that either of them can speak amazing English at all is a shock to me learning and speaking English is not part of the culture and inent Cal Asia there is no English language influence so it's just not important less than 5% of the population can conver fluently in English this is one of the lowest English speaking rates in the world most restaurants will not have English menus therefore Google Translate is going to be your friend everywhere here USC people generally Converse in USBC outside of tashkin especially that will be people's preferred language however 90% of people do speak Russian and nearly all can understand some basic Russian this this is because usbekistan was part of the former Soviet Union so if you can speak Russian well you'll get along fine but learning a few USC phrases and words wouldn't hurt as well also do not be surprised if people assume that you are Russian or have Russian Heritage because there's a sizable Russian population that live in usbekistan and also many torist visit from Russia as well also don't be surprised if people when they find out you're from Western Europe or the USA for example you become an instant Rockstar and people want to meet you and speak to you this happened to me on a road trip to the regional city of fera from tashkin at a service station in fera Valley the local woman who was running an USC breadstand called all of her kids to come and meet me after I came to her stall dealer explained to me that some of the local USCS may have never met a Wester before so it was kind of a big deal to them at the time number five something you will need to know if you're not staying in a hotel so for example if you're staying in rented accommodation or with some locals is that you will need to register your arrival and location with the police in the days after you enter obcam you can actually do this online or in person at a local police station All Foreign citizens visiting usbekistan on a temporary basis whether they need a Visa or not must submit documents for registration where they are staying within 72 hours registration in usbekistan is essential and unavoidable to register online you will need to go on the Emon website fill out the form and at the end pay the tourist tax it costs around $20 for up to one month you can also do this as well in person but it is an annoying process as you will need to physically visit the police station get an appointment wait for the appointment and then fight other foreigners to keep your place in the queue the whole process can take a couple of hours so bear that in mind it's definitely easier to do it online if you're staying in a hotel they will sort this out for you so you don't need to worry number six and to be honest with you this is actually the number one reason to visit usbekistan in the first place usbekistan is the jewel in the Silk Road crown the region has a truly glorious past and has at times been the Envy of the world in terms of its wealth cities trade routs and culture this makes it a must visit location at least once in my opinion to appreciate this glorious past and understand the impact usbekistan has had on the world in modern times usbekistan is not a well-known country although it has contributed to Major advances in science medicine architecture and literature number seven speaking of reasons to visit usbekistan there are some genuinely world-class tourist attractions like registan square and the best part is all the attractions we visited have very small number of tourists some places were genuinely empty and we were the only tourists there this meant we could take our time and capture some amazing photos imagine being at the Eiffel Tower or Bingham Palace and having the place to yourselves it will actually be this reality in usbekistan we visited during a national holiday and it was still relatively quiet everywhere we went so I'd recommend any time of the year to visit usbekistan number eight and something else which is amazing from a tourist perspective is how cheap the country is being one of the cheapest in the world for example the Metro is pennies for a ride taxis will cost you a couple of dollars for a standard journey and a meal in a decent restaurant can be as little as $5 a person the currency in usbekistan is USC SS and due to inflation you will see some crazy denominations for example 1 million s is about £70 or $100 you can truly have a fstar experience here for a relatively small amount of money for example I took dealer's family out for the day at a luxury hotel where we had an UNP barbecue swimming pool enough food and alcohol for 12 people and it cost me £120 or $150 something like that in the UK would have been at least 6 or700 what's great about Z bistan is you could probably budget for food transport and experiences around $25 per person per day and have an amazing experience so it's really great if you're on a budget the flight to usbekistan is probably going to be the biggest expense of your trip so once you actually in the country and you're here and you're having a good time it's actually really cheap Yandex taxis I mentioned how cheap usbekistan is where I found it to be crazy cheap is the taxis in the UK a 20-minute taxi ride can cost 3040 in usbekistan that same trip can be as cheap as 1 to2 per trip don't believe me download the Yandex app the Yandex app is the Uber of usbekistan and put in a trip from tashkin airport to the center you will see some crazy low prices the app is fantastic it is is used the friendly as Uber and in English so the language barrier when it comes to travel here is not an issue you can even pick Comfort class and drive around in a BMW or a Tesla for a tiny amount of money imagine swanning around in style with your own personal driver it is genuinely that cheap so you probably thinking why is it that cheap well one of the reasons is the fuel methane is used for a lot of the vehicles as opposed to petrol or diesel you will see large gas tanks strapped underneath trucks for example because of this number 10 speaking of driving in usbekistan I've been to India China turkey Italy Thailand all famous for crazy driving and Roads nothing and I mean nothing compared to bistan the driving is wild in a city like Mumbai but because the traffic is bad cars are moving slowly and speed isn't a massive issue in The Pedestrian areas this is not the case in zekan the scary thing about usbekistan is they have large wide roads and the traffic is not so bad so cars will speed up to 50 60 70 mph in the city center pedestrians are treated as second class citizens on the road it is genuinely dangerous to walk around the road so I'd recommend sticking to the path and using taxis where possible you will see really crazy things like a car speeding up to a crazy level only to find there's nowhere to go and then have to slam the brakes on so harshly smoke bows from the wheel arches another terrifying experience I had was on the way to ferger we were stuck in traffic and a bus got kind of bored of waiting a public bus that is and it literally just sped past us and went onto the side of the road onto the curb and as it hit the curb the bus actually took off into the air I have never seen this before I mean it must have been going I don't know 40 50 m hour it must have been terrifying to be on that bus but yeah it was really strange and weird to see generally the driving is just crazy I was joking with deala that visiting as a tourist and seeing it all play out is like being in a Mario Kart race but no one seems to agree where the finish line is here driving on the road is like a competitive Sport and a truly unique experience I do not not recommend renting a car use taxis for small distances or trains for inter city travel the interesting thing is most of the people here seem very proud of their cars and unlike other places with crazy driving like turkey for example where the cars can be all scuffed up here often the cars look brand new and are an Immaculate condition number 11 and speaking of cars observing cars on the road is super interesting as the tourist visiting because you'll notice something very quickly the vast majority of the cars are Chevrolet in fact 94% of new car s come from the American manufacturer this is because General Motors partnered with the OBC government back in the late 2000s to run manufacturing plants in usbekistan so it is the local manufacturer I suppose USCS also like Chevrolets as the roads in usbekistan are rough and bumpy and being mostly desert usbekistan is a harsh environment and the cars need to be able to cope Chevrolets are durable and relatively cheap to maintain with parts easily accessible due to those manufacturing plants I was talking about furthermore there is a super harsh import tax on cars from overseas for example if you wanted to import a Mercedes S-Class it would cost in the range of $660,000 just to get into the country this is due to high customs duty and vat this is remarkable when you consider the car itself is worth around $100,000 so it's costing you the price of the car and then another 60% on top just to drive in usbekistan your Mercedes S-Class therefore driving a foreign car in usbekistan is a real status symbol the other 6% of cars are typically the Chinese brand byd or Soviet era laders which along with Japanese taxis are a bit of a guilty pleasure of mine to be honest there is something really funny about seeing a 50-year-old ladder against the modern backdrop of tashkin so there you have it 11 things you should know about usbekistan some of them you probably heard before but I'm hoping some of them are a bit unique and a bit quirky that you've probably not heard I have got a few more of these and some more kind of weird ones so I'll definitely do probably a second video where I Do cover it but anyway thanks for watching and we'll see you in the next one

recommendations: Whether you're visiting Samarkand, Tashkent, Bukhara, or Khiva, these 11 must-know travel tips will help you get the best experience

videos.title: Tashkent, Uzbekistan: Best Things to Do & See | Travel Guide
videos.description:
Planning a trip to Tashkent, Uzbekistan in 2026? This Tashkent travel guide covers the best things to do, hidden gems, and essential travel tips for Central Asia’s greenest capital.

Tashkent, the capital of Uzbekistan, is the largest city in Central Asia and where most journeys to the country begin. Unlike the famous Silk Road cities of Samarkand, Bukhara, and Khiva, Tashkent often gets overlooked — many travellers just fly in and out. But this city is definitely worth a stop. Rebuilt after a devastating earthquake in 1966, Tashkent feels modern at first glance, with wide boulevards and Soviet-style architecture, yet it’s also one of the greenest cities in the region and full of hidden gems.

In this video, we share the best things to do and see in Tashkent, along with travel tips to help you make the most of your visit. We take you to Independence Square, the Monument to the Grieving Mother, and the lively Amir Timur Square, before wandering along Sayilgoh Street (Broadway) for street food and trying manti, one of Uzbekistan’s national dishes. We also explore the colourful Seoul Mun District, visit the traditional Chorsu Bazaar, and step inside the Hazrat Imam Complex, home to one of the oldest Qurans in the world. Along the way, we share practical tips — from using Yandex taxis to quirky observations, like why most cars in Tashkent are white.

videos.transcript: [Music] Welcome to Tashkan in Usbakistan. Tashkan might not be as famous as Samaran or Bkara, but it's the capital of Usbakistan and the largest city in Central Asia where most journeys begin. At first glance, it feels modern with wide blue and Soviet style architecture. A look that comes from being almost completely rebuilt after a devastating earthquake in 1966. But once you start exploring, you will find colorful bazars, historic mosque, and lively squares that reveal its unique character. Let's not forget that Tashka is one of the greenest city in the region with endless parks and threeline avenues. A lot of people when they come to Pakistan, they skip Tashkan and they just fly in and fly out from Tashkan. And I understand why because they want to use their time to see all the historic towns like Spokara, Saman. And also they want to go and see the main stops on the S route. But if you have time, you can come to Taja and before flying out, you can stay a day and explore the city a bit. We spent the day in Tashkan when we first arrived in Osbakistan before flying to Kiba. Welcome to Bkaro. And then after visiting the historical parts of the country and spending time in a Y camp. >> This is our Y. >> We came back and decided to spend more days here before flying back home. In this video, I will take you through the best things to do in Tashkan, share some travel tips, and show you why you should add Tashkan in your itinary. Wow, this is beautiful. There are so many fountains around the city. [Music] [Applause] The independent square is the main square in Kashkan. This square is not the small one. It's a huge park and you will see a lot of security and police around here because lots of state buildings are located here. Independence Square is the largest square in Usbakistan and it's not just the political heart of Tashkan. It's also a popular place for locals to walk and relax. This square is decorated with beautiful fountains, gardens, and wide open spaces which make it feel calm and welcoming despite the surrounding official buildings. [Music] Just next to the square, you will find a monument to the grieving mother, also known as the crying mother monument. It was built in the memory of the soldiers who died during World War II. And in front of the statues burn an eternal flame, a symbol of remembrance that never goes out. The monument represents the sorrow of countless mothers who lost their sons in the war. And it's a place where people still come to pay their respects today. [Music] [Applause] [Music] If you're coming to Tashkan or in general in Pakistan, you need to download Yandex app which works as Uber. And whenever you want to get the taxi, you can just book a taxi on the app and you can pay it in cash. So it save you a lot of hassle of negotiating and there are not a lot of taxi around. Actually a lot of them are Yandex. So it's easier. >> Actually we had some issues with Yandex when we came here because we installed it while we were here and we couldn't activate it with our UK number. We were not receiving the the text the verifi for verification. Uh, so I think it only works if you have like the USB number here. And what we ended up doing, we because we had eim cards, but RC E SIM cards were data only. So we didn't have a phone number, so we couldn't activate the app. You actually have to go into a shop and get a a local SIM card in order to activate it. So maybe it's a good idea to install Yandex while you're away and activate it before coming here. So you don't have to do that. [Music] Amir Tamur Square is one of the main squares in Tashkan and it is named after Amir Tamur who was the founder of Timurid dynasty in Usbakistan and Central Asia and some part of also Iran and Afghanistan. And honestly, after visiting his mausoleium in Samaran, we now understand just how much Usbeks admire him. Tamu built the vast empire stretching across central Asia, Persia and beyond. At the time, Samaran was his capital, and he dreamt of making it the most beautiful city in the world. In many ways, he succeeded. The monuments he left behind are still some of the finest examples of Islamic architecture. Here in Tashkan is the statue surrounded by grand buildings, gardens and wide boulevards. And also around the square is like a park. A lot of people, locals come here just to relax, read books and some tourists can come here too because it's nice and when it's hot is you want to be on the shade. So it's good to come here if you are in Teskan. [Music] One thing I noticed in Teskan and in general was Pakistan that a lot of workers they keep cleaning the roads and parks and everywhere actually and they use the traditional and old style brooms and a lot of them are women actually. From Amitu Square is just a short walk to Sailor Street, also known as Broadway. This pedestrian street is one of the most popular places in Tashka for both locals and visitors. Lined with trees, fountains, coffees, and little stars. It is also a great spot to try some of Tashkan's street food. By chance, we discovered this street and it's full of street food. And I want to try this one is potato. This one has meat. And this one is chicken sausage. And this one is mixed meat. So >> which one I should try? I want to try one. >> Which one is the best? This one is good. I go for the chicken. So one. Yeah. One. Thank you. How much is that? 5,000 some which is not even£1. [Music] Actually, this one is called Pashki. And we have something similar in Iran too. And it's like a donut. Then you fry the dough and you put inside something like uh in Iran can be sweet like you like a donut like you put custard and fry it. So let's try. It tastes like brashki. It's good. It remind me of my childhood. [Music] >> It has a relaxed atmosphere during the day and really comes alive in the evening. Apparently here they love Pepsi. You can see the Pepsi sign almost everywhere. [Music] For the first meal in Usbakistan, actually in Tashan, we went for the national dish. It's manti. One is just boiled wine and the other one is fried wine. We tried something similar in Georgia and Armenia. But let's try this one. In Georgia and Armenia, they offer it with pork, beef, and I think chicken. But here it was just beef. In Georgia they told us we should eat it. We should suck the juice. I want to just try to see are they different. It's so tasty. I think the difference is that there we had to suck the juice out but here we can just eat it and it's really tasty. Manti was actually our very first meal in Usbakistan and since then we've tried it in different parts of the country but honestly the best one was at the York camp where we even had a chance to make it and the one we had here in Tashka. [Music] Another interesting place to see in Tashka is the Sunmoon District. One of the most lively areas in Tashkan is here, Soul Moon. It's a modern area inspired by Korean architecture with colorful cafes and a playful vibe that feels very different from the historical parts of the city. There are plenty of restaurants here, but what we noticed is that many of them are more like fast food places. That's why we came here mainly for a visit rather than to eat. If you're looking for proper respect meals or international cuisine, you will find much better option in other parts of the city. During the day, this place is not very busy. But when the sun goes down, this place comes to life. The other night, we came here and it was very busy and we didn't have any camera with us. We just wanted to rest. But today is going to rain. That's why it's not very busy right now. Vashka has plenty of modern shopping malls. The traditional bazaar is still very much alive. The most famous one is Chu Bazar, a massive dome market that's been at the center of trade for centuries. This is the main bazar in Tashkan. And actually, it's not a tourist attraction. is where locals come and buy fresh produce, clothes, anything, meat, anything they need, they come to this bazaar. The word choo actually means crossroads. And that's exactly what this place has always been, a meeting point for traders and locals. >> This is a tea. It's so fruity and flowery. Smells so good. He's giving me another one to just a smell. >> This is normal. [Music] I can see that is a strawberry inside [Music] spice fix for >> actually soup. >> This is for soup. >> This is for soup. Chili. >> Yeah. >> For sil. >> Yeah. about forces for >> when he was explaining me then he started talking in forcy and as you know I'mian so I can speak far but he's back he's from Usbakistan but from Saman in Tashkan not many people speak far but when you go to Bkharo and Saman most of the people there speak far Oh my god. This is in Persian we call it gorgeous salad and it actually sour tomato and they are always this size and green and they're very sour. You should eat them with salt and they're very tasty and you can find them only in spring. [Music] 25. Thank you. I just got white malberry that usually I don't find uh this kind of fruits in the UK. So, it's nice to see it and be in a rice season here and have them. If you never try them, they are very sweet and very delicious. >> It tastes like my chai, too. The Bazar's huge blue dome was built in the 1980s. It has become one of the Tashkans landmarks. [Music] Usually when you travel most of the souvenir look like each other but here are so different and all of the statues have like traditional clothes of Pakistan and chess is a big thing in this country and you can see it in the civil actually one thing I noticed here I don't know if it's just me but there's a lot cars that are white and the rest are mainly black or gray. There's no like colorful cars at all. So, we don't know if there's a reason behind it. It just looks weird looking at the road. Just mainly white cars. Most cars, not just in Tashkan, but across the whole country are white. Unlike neighboring country Turkmanistan where there are actual restrictions on car colors, Usbakistan doesn't have such laws. He had a preference for white is purely practical. Lighter cars stay cooler during the summer months when temperatures can rise over 35°. White paint is also cheaper and it is considered more convenient for this set. So, it's no wonder the streets are almost entirely filled with white cars. One of the most beautiful places to visit in Tashka is the Hazat Imam complex also known as K IM square. This is the religious heart of the city. Home to several historic mosque and madestas. The highlight here is the Muim Mouak library which houses one of the oldest Qurans in the world dating back to the 7th century. Walking around the complex feels peaceful and calm, especially compared to the busiest street trees and bazar. Actually, this is the tree of white malbury. Usually, you can just come here and grab them if uh they're ready to be eaten. Tashkan might not have the fame of Saman or Bkhara, but it has its own charm. It was the perfect way to both start and finish our Usbakistan journey. If you want to see more from this amazing country, check out my other videos from Samaran Bkhara Fivo and our time in a Y camp. But for now, same as always, thanks for watching and see you in the next video. [Music]

I recommend heading to the outer rim to find the non-bakers first, watching them pull hot stamped bread from the clay ovens as a show in itself, and a steaming loaf costs around 50ents
Afterward, check out the stalls of colorful e-cot fabrics or traditional knives
Check out the Barack Khan Madrasa on the side of the square
I recommend taking a loop around the park to see the contrast between the imperial history and the modern forum palace next door
It's the best spot to see how the local cuisine stands up in a fine dining environment
To reach your next stop, forget the taxi and head underground
This is the best spot in the capital to people watch and see the modern youth culture of Usbekiststan
You might even share a table with a local family, which is honestly the best way to experience hospitality
It brings a great balance of savory and sweet with chickpeas, raisins, and quail eggs piled on top
Make sure you stop by the open kitchen before you eat
I strongly recommend you hire a private driver for this
